objectives: Identify the inputs and outputs of a routine, distinguishing them from internal processes.
Explain preconditions, caching and reusable components, including the benefits and trade-offs of caching.
Apply thinking ahead to plan an unseen routine before coding it.

retrieval.a: A condition that must be true before a routine can run correctly, e.g. a list sorted before binary search.

retrieval.q: Give the main benefit and main trade-off of caching.
retrieval.a: Benefit: faster reuse with less recompute/traffic. Trade-off: extra storage and data can become stale.

retrieval.q: Name two benefits of a reusable component.
retrieval.a: Faster development and fewer bugs because it is pre-tested; also consistency and easier maintenance.

retrieval.q: Why is 'validate the data' an input/output trap?
retrieval.a: It is a process that transforms data, not data flowing in or a result flowing out.

retrieval.q: Interleaving (2.1.1): how does abstraction differ from thinking ahead?
retrieval.a: Abstraction removes irrelevant detail to simplify a model; thinking ahead plans inputs, outputs, preconditions and reuse before coding.

keywords.def: Data passed into a routine, e.g. quantity and delivery address for an order.
A result produced by a routine, e.g. dispensed cash and an updated balance.
A step that transforms data inside a routine; not an input or output.
A condition that must be true before a routine runs correctly.
Storing an expensive or frequently used result in fast storage for quick reuse.
Cached data that is out of date because the source has changed since it was stored.
Refreshing or discarding cached data so stale values are not served.
A self-contained, generalised, pre-tested module that can be dropped into many programs.

content.heading: Inputs and outputs
points: Inputs are data in; outputs are results out.
Internal processes (e.g. validating, applying a discount) are neither.
Listing real inputs/outputs first shapes the rest of the design.

content.heading: Preconditions
points: A condition that must hold before a routine runs correctly.
Example: a list must be sorted before binary search will work.
If unchecked, the routine may give wrong results or fail.

content.heading: Caching
points: Store expensive/frequent results in fast storage for reuse.
Benefit: faster responses and less recomputation or network traffic.
Trade-off: extra storage and data can go stale, so it needs invalidation.

content.heading: Reusable components
points: Self-contained, generalised modules used across many programs.
Benefits: faster development, fewer bugs (pre-tested), consistency.
Also easier maintenance — fix once and every user benefits.

content.heading: Exam technique
points: Name actual inputs/outputs, the precondition, what to cache and what to reuse.
When asked to discuss caching, give both a benefit and a trade-off.
Apply to the scenario — generic definitions score poorly.

worked_example.title: Planning a 'display personalised news feed' routine before coding
steps: Inputs: the user ID, their follow/friend list and post timestamps.
Output: an ordered list of posts rendered to the feed.
Precondition: the user must be logged in and their profile loaded before a personalised feed can be built.
Caching: cache recently fetched posts so reopening the app is fast and server requests drop.
Trade-off of that cache: it can go stale, so it must be refreshed/invalidated periodically.
Reusable component: reuse an existing date/time-formatting or image-loading module instead of rewriting it, saving time and bugs.

task: A flight-booking website is being planned. For its 'calculate total price' routine, identify two inputs and one output, state one precondition, and decide one value worth caching — giving one benefit and one trade-off of caching it in this scenario.
challenge: A company is building three apps that all need date validation. Justify writing it as one reusable component rather than three copies, then explain one situation where a shared component could be a disadvantage.

exit_ticket.q: In an online shop's 'place order' routine, classify: the customer's address, the confirmation email, validating the card number.
exit_ticket.a: Address = input; confirmation email = output; validating the card number = process (neither).

exit_ticket.q: Why must a login routine run before the account page is shown, and what could go wrong if it didn't?
exit_ticket.a: Authentication is a precondition; without it an unauthenticated user could see private data or the page could error on missing data.

exit_ticket.q: State one benefit and one trade-off of caching currency rates on a booking site.
exit_ticket.a: Benefit: rates load fast from local storage, reducing traffic. Trade-off: rates can go stale and quote out-of-date prices unless invalidated.
